summary: China has announced plans to implement a comprehensive citizen scoring system using big data, developed in partnership with tech giants Alibaba, Baidu, and Tencent. The system expands beyond traditional credit scores to include purchasing behavior, social connections, and political alignment. While Western observers frame this as uniquely Chinese authoritarianism, O'Neil argues that similar 'social credit scoring' mechanisms are already being implemented in the United States through alternative lending and reputation systems. The post challenges the assumption that such surveillance-based scoring is unimaginable in Western democracies.
key_points: China's citizen scoring system incorporates online behavior, purchases, social networks, and political loyalty into a single reputation score
Major Chinese tech companies (Alibaba, Baidu, Tencent) are partnering with the government to build this surveillance infrastructure
A Chinese official justified the system by arguing that when morality doesn't constrain behavior, systems must restrict actions instead
Western observers claim such government-corporate data exploitation is 'unimaginable' in the West
O'Neil contends that similar social credit scoring is already being implemented in the US through alternative lending practices
money_quotes: When people's behavior isn't bound by their morality, a system must be used to restrict their actions
Government and big internet companies in China can exploit 'Big Data' together in a way that is unimaginable in the West
I guess I'm wondering whether that's really true
I see this kind of 'social credit scoring' being widely implemented here in the United States
key_insight: The dystopian citizen scoring system China is building openly may already be emerging in subtler forms in the United States through private-sector data exploitation.
